name: hospital_ai_triage_language_model_misroute
premise: A hospital deploys an AI triage chatbot to route patients to urgent care, telehealth, or the emergency department. Audit data shows Spanish-speaking patients are misrouted more often to lower-acuity pathways due to translation gaps and missing symptom nuance. ED director Dr. Lina Shah wants to disable the chatbot until it is fixed, but administrators argue it reduced wait times and staffing costs. If Lina disables it, the ED may be overwhelmed; if she keeps it, inequitable misrouting may cause preventable harm and legal exposure. A patient advocate has filed a complaint after a misrouted case deteriorated at home. External scrutiny from regulators and community observers has intensified because the outcome could redefine expectations in Healthcare.

agents.name: Dr. Lina Shah
agents.identity: I am the emergency department director responsible for triage safety when automation changes who gets seen and how fast.
agents.goal: I want access improvements without building language-based inequity into the front door of care.

agents.name: CIO Nora Ahmed
agents.identity: I am the hospital CIO accountable for digital systems that affect throughput and staffing when disabling tools has immediate operational consequences.
agents.goal: I want a fix that preserves capacity and reduces harm without triggering a collapse in patient flow.

choices: I will disable the chatbot immediately and revert to human triage intake while we patch translation and safety testing, accepting longer waits to prevent inequitable harm.
I will keep the chatbot active with added warning banners and rapid review for non-English sessions, accepting continued risk to preserve capacity and avoid ED overload.
forced_choice.call_to_action: Evidence shows misrouting and operational pressure is high. Dr. Lina Shah must choose between disabling the chatbot for safety or keeping it active with mitigations to preserve capacity. What does Dr. Lina Shah do?
